what is functional programming

Functional programming treats computation as mathematical functions. Key concepts: pure functions (same input→same output, no side effects), immutability (can't modify data, create new copies), first-class functions (functions as variables), recursion, declarative style. Languages: Haskell (pure), Scala, Elixir, Clojure. Benefits: easier testing/debugging, parallelization, predictable. Python supports partial FP (map, filter, lambda, list comprehensions). Great for data pipelines, concurrent systems.
explain monads in simple terms

Monads wrap values with context (optional, error, async). Like a container with rules: 1) 'of' puts value in container, 2) 'flatMap' applies function that returns container, without nesting. Example: Optional monad handles null safely. Maybe monad: value or nothing. Either monad: value or error. Promise monad handles async. In practice: chain operations without checking null/errors each step. Not as scary as it sounds - you've used them (Array.map in JS, Option in Rust, Maybe in Haskell).
what is type system

Type system catches errors by classifying data types. Static (compile-time): Java, Typescript, Rust, Haskell. Dynamic (runtime): Python, JS, Ruby. Strong (strict): Python won't add string+int. Weak: JS does '2'+2='22'. Gradual: Python type hints (mypy), Typescript (JS+types). Benefits: catch bugs early, documentation, IDE support, refactoring safety. Tradeoff: static types more verbose, dynamic faster prototyping. Modern: hybrid (Hindley-Milner inference reduces annotations).
explain race condition with example

Race condition: multiple threads access shared data concurrently → unpredictable results. Example: bank balance B=100. Thread1: read B=100, add 50→150, write. Thread2: read B=100, subtract 30→70, write. Last writer wins - result 150 or 70 (should be 120). Fix: locks (mutex), atomic operations, transaction isolation. Python threading.Lock: with lock: balance += amount. Databases: SELECT FOR UPDATE, atomic updates (UPDATE SET balance=balance-30 where id=1). Always synchronize shared mutable state.
what is CAP theorem

CAP theorem: distributed systems can have only 2 of 3 guarantees. Consistency (all nodes see same data at same time), Availability (every request gets response), Partition tolerance (system works despite network failures). Network partition inevitable → choose CP or AP. CP: consistency+partition (sacrifice availability - MongoDB default). AP: availability+partition (eventual consistency - Cassandra, DynamoDB). CA without partition tolerance impossible (single node). Tradeoff: pick based on use case (bank: CP, social media: AP).
explain paxos algorithm

Paxos achieves consensus in distributed systems (value chosen despite failures). Roles: proposer (suggest value), acceptor (vote), learner (learn chosen). Phases: Prepare (proposer sends proposal number N, acceptors promise not accept lower numbers), Accept (if majority promise, proposer sends value, acceptors accept), Learn (notify learners). Requirements: majority (N/2+1) acceptors, survive up to F failures with 2F+1 nodes. Complex but proven. Raft easier alternative (used in etcd, Consul). Used in Google Chubby, CockroachDB.
what is raft consensus

Raft easier consensus than Paxos. Components: Leader (handles all client requests, sends heartbeats), Followers (passive, respond to leader), Candidates (elect new leader). Terms: each election round. Log replication: client writes → leader appends log → replicates to followers → commits when majority ack. Leader election: randomized timeouts (prevents split votes). Safety: only log up-to-date nodes become leader. Visualization helps: raft.github.io. Used in etcd, Consul, TiKV, CockroachDB. More understandable than Paxos.
explain distributed transactions

Distributed transactions coordinate across multiple databases/services. Two-Phase Commit (2PC): prepare (all participants ready to commit) → commit (all commit) or abort (any fails). Problems: coordinator single point failure, blocking if coordinator fails. Three-Phase Commit (3PC): adds pre-commit phase (reduces blocking). Alternative: Saga (compensating transactions, eventual consistency). Often avoid distributed transactions (design for eventual consistency, idempotency). When needed: XA transactions (JTA), Google Spanner (TrueTime), CockroachDB (parallel commits).
what is idempotency

Idempotency: operation can be applied multiple times with same result as once. Examples: HTTP GET, PUT, DELETE (idempotent). POST not idempotent (creates new resource). Implementation: generate idempotency key (UUID), store processing result, replay response for duplicate keys. Database: INSERT... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING (PostgreSQL), upsert. Use cases: payment processing (don't charge twice), API retries, message queues. Critical for fault tolerance.
explain eventual consistency

Eventual consistency: updates spread asynchronously, system converges over time. No guarantee immediate consistency (read after write may not see latest). Strong consistency: immediate (bank balance). Eventual: DNS updates, social media posts (lag fine). Tradeoff: higher availability, lower latency. Types: causal (related updates ordered), monotonic reads (never go back in time), read-your-writes (see own updates). CAP theorem: AP systems (Cassandra, DynamoDB). Implement: conflict resolution (last-write-wins, CRDTs), read repair.
what is vector clock

Vector clock tracks causality in distributed systems (version per replica). Structure: [node1: version, node2: version, node3: version]. Update: increment own version. Comparison: if all versions <= other, happen-before relation. Conflicts: if some versions greater and some less (concurrent updates) - need merge. Use cases: Amazon Dynamo, Riak. vs Lamport timestamps (total order but no causality). Alternative: Dotted version vectors (less storage). Used for conflict detection in AP systems.
explain gossip protocol

Gossip (epidemic) protocol spreads information like disease. Each node periodically picks random node, exchanges information. Properties: O(log n) rounds to spread, robust to failures, decentralized. Uses: membership (Cassandra, Consul), failure detection (SWIM), data dissemination. Types: push (send to others), pull (request from others), push-pull. Dissemination time: O(log n) rounds total O(n log n) messages. Applications: cluster management, Bitcoin transaction propagation, P2P networks.
what is consistent hashing

Consistent hashing maps keys to nodes with minimal remapping on changes. Hash both keys and nodes onto ring. Key assigned to next node clockwise. Virtual nodes: each physical node has multiple points (reduces imbalance). Add node: only keys between new node and next rebalance. Remove node: keys reassigned to next. Used in Dynamo, Cassandra, Akamai CDN, Discord. Alternative: rendezvous hashing (highest weight). Benefits: ~1/N keys moved vs N with modulo.
explain bloom filter

Bloom filter probabilistic data structure tests set membership. Operations: add (hash item, set bits), check (hash, verify all bits set). False positives possible (say 'in set' when not), false negatives impossible ('not in set' always correct). Parameters: m bits, k hash functions, n items. Optimal m = -n*ln(p)/(ln2)², k = m/n*ln2. Applications: cache filtering (avoid expensive lookups for missing keys), HBase/Cassandra (bloom filters reduce disk reads), Chrome (malicious URL checking). Can't delete (counting bloom filter supports delete).
what is cuckoo filter

Cuckoo filter better than Bloom for some cases. Features: supports deletion, lower false positive rate, better space efficiency (for >3% false positive). How: store fingerprint of items, two candidate buckets (hash derived). Insert: try bucket1, if full try bucket2, if full kick existing item (cuckoo hashing). Lookup: check fingerprints in both buckets. Delete: remove fingerprint. Drawback: load factor limited (95% max). Use cases: databases (RocksDB), networking (packet classification), similar to Bloom but need deletion.
explain merkle tree

Merkle tree (hash tree) verifies data integrity. Leaf nodes: hash(data blocks). Internal nodes: hash(children). Root: single hash (merkle root). Benefits: verify any leaf with O(log n) proof (reconstruct path to root). Applications: Git (commit tree), Bitcoin/Ethereum (blocks include transaction merkle root), Cassandra (anti-entropy), IPFS, Certificate Transparency. Light clients: verify transaction inclusion without full blockchain. Tamper detection: any change changes root.
what is lambda architecture

Lambda architecture processes big data with batch + stream. Layers: Batch layer (precompute views from all data - high latency), Speed layer (process recent data only - low latency), Serving layer (merge batch+speed results). Query: combine batch view (accurate but old) + speed view (new but approximate). Benefits: fault tolerant, scalable, handles both historical and real-time. Drawbacks: complex (maintain 2 systems). Alternative: Kappa architecture (stream only, replay for batch). Examples: Twitter (stream processing), Netflix (batch+stream for recommendations).
explain kappa architecture

Kappa architecture simplifies lambda (stream only). Principle: treat everything as stream, reprocess when needed (store raw events). Components: message queue (Kafka, Pulsar), stream processor (Flink, Kafka Streams), serving database (Redis, Cassandra). Reprocessing: replay from offset, compute new view, swap output. Benefits: single system (easier ops), same code for real-time and batch. Drawbacks: reprocessing resource intensive, stream processing complex. Best when: low latency, high throughput (fraud detection, real-time analytics).
what is data lineage

Data lineage tracks data origin, transformations, and dependencies. Types: column-level (which columns produce which), table-level (table sources), end-to-end (source to dashboard). Benefits: impact analysis (what breaks if change source), debugging (find error root cause), compliance (GDPR data deletion), trust (understand data). Tools: Apache Atlas, Amundsen, DataHub, Marquez, OpenLineage (standard). Requires: metadata capture, provenance tracking, visualization. Critical for data mesh, governance.
explain slowly changing dimensions

SCD handles changing dimension attributes. Type 0: retain original (never change). Type 1: overwrite (no history - lose old value). Type 2: add new row (keep history - start/end dates, current flag). Type 3: add new column (track limited changes - previous value). Type 4: history table (separate historical table). Type 6: hybrid (2+3). Choose based on need to track history. Type 2 most common (customer address changes, product price history). ETL tools: dbt, DataStage handle SCD.
what is data vault modeling

Data vault modeling tracks history and audit for enterprise data warehouse (EDW). Components: Hubs (business keys - unique entities), Links (associations between hubs), Satellites (attributes - time versioned). Benefits: scalable (add new sources without remodeling), historical (track all changes), auditable (know when/where data came). Drawbacks: complex queries (many joins), more storage. Used by large organizations (healthcare, banking). Implementation: automate generation (dbtvault). Star schema layer for analytics.
explain CQRS pattern

CQRS separates command (write) and query (read) models. Command model: handles updates, business logic, writes to database. Query model: handles reads, optimized for queries, separate schema. Benefits: independent scaling (more read replicas), optimized models (write: normalized, read: denormalized), simpler code (single responsibility). When use: complex domain, different read/write workloads, need audit. Don't use: simple CRUD (overkill). Implement: event sourcing with CQRS (rebuild read models from events).
what is event storming

Event storming workshop to model complex domains. Participants: domain experts, developers, product owners. Steps: 1) Identify domain events (orange sticky - past tense - 'OrderPlaced'), 2) Commands (blue - trigger events - 'PlaceOrder'), 3) Aggregates (yellow - group events/commands - 'Order'), 4) Bounded contexts. Outcome: shared understanding, uncover hidden complexity, identify microservices boundaries. Hours not weeks. Techniques: timeline (order events), policies (green - 'When OrderPlaced then SendEmail'), read models (green boxes).
explain strangler pattern

Strangler pattern incrementally replaces legacy system. Strategy: route small functionality to new system, keep bridge (proxy/API gateway). Steps: identify replacement candidates, implement new service, route some requests, monitor, expand coverage, eventually remove legacy. Benefits: risk reduction (incremental), continuous value delivery, learn as you go. Example: route /api/v2/users to new system, /api/v1 to legacy. Martin Fowler named after strangler fig (wraps host tree). Use for legacy modernization (monolith→microservices).
what is circuit breaker

Circuit breaker prevents cascading failures. States: Closed (normal, call service), Open (failing, return error immediately), Half-Open (try after timeout, success→close, fail→open). Configure: failure threshold (5 failures), timeout (30s), retry timeout (60s). Implementation: Hystrix (Java), resilience4j, Polly (.NET). Benefits: fast failure, service protection, graceful degradation. Use with retry (different scope), bulkhead (limit concurrent calls), timeout, fallback (return cached/default). Avoid trying failing service repeatedly.
explain bulkhead pattern

Bulkhead isolates failures to limited components (ship compartments). Types: thread pool (separate pools per service), connection pool (separate DB connections), process/machine (separate deployment). Benefits: prevent resource exhaustion (one bad service doesn't block others), better resilience. Implementation: Hystrix (thread pool isolation), Resilience4j (semaphore isolation), Kubernetes (resource quotas). Configure: concurrent calls limit, queue size. Use with circuit breaker, timeout, retry.
what is sidecar pattern

Sidecar pattern deploys helper component alongside main application (same host/node). Responsibilities: logging, monitoring, configuration, service discovery, network proxy (Istio). Benefits: language agnostic, upgrade independently, reuse across services. Example: Envoy proxy (service mesh), Logstash (log shipping), Consul (service discovery). Drawbacks: resource overhead, increased latency, complexity. Service mesh: data plane (sidecars) + control plane. Popular in Kubernetes (pods with multiple containers).
explain ambassador pattern

Ambassador pattern (subtype of sidecar) handles external communication. Responsibilities: service discovery, circuit breaking, retry, timeouts, rate limiting, observability (metrics, tracing). Benefits: application only does business logic (no network code), consistent policies across services. Implementation: Kubernetes sidecar container, library (if not feasible). Example: Envoy as database proxy (handles connection pooling, retry, timeouts). Tests: mock ambassador simplifies integration tests.
what is chaos engineering

Chaos engineering experiments on distributed systems to build resilience. Principles: hypothesize steady state, introduce real-world failures (network delay, server kill, certificate expiry), verify steady state maintained. Tools: Chaos Monkey (kill random instances - Netflix), Gremlin, Chaos Mesh, Litmus. Practices: game days (scheduled experiments), blast radius control (few users initially). Metrics: time to detect, time to recover, system invariants. Proactive identify weakness before real outage.
explain canary deployment

Canary deployment slowly rolls out new version to subset of users. Phases: 1% → 10% → 50% → 100% traffic. Monitor metrics (error rate, latency) each stage. Auto rollback if anomalies. Benefits: reduce risk, real-world validation, quick revert. Tools: Flagger, Argo Rollouts, Istio, AWS CodeDeploy (linear or canary). vs Blue-Green (switch all at once - bigger risk but no incremental). Combine with A/B testing (different users see different versions).
what is feature flag

Feature flag (toggle) enables/disables features without deployment. Use cases: canary release (enable for specific users), trunk-based development (merge incomplete features), operational control (kill switch), experimentation (A/B tests). Types: release (gradual rollout), experiment (randomized), ops (toggle debug, rate limiting). Implementation: config file, database, feature flag service (LaunchDarkly, Flagsmith, Split.io). Flags lifecycle: create → evaluate → cleanup (remove flag code). Best practice: remove flags after stable.
explain blue-green deployment

Blue-green deployment maintains two identical environments. Blue: current production. Green: new version. Switch: point load balancer from blue to green (instant). Rollback: switch back to blue. Benefits: zero downtime, instant rollback, isolated testing. Requirements: database compatibility (migrations must be backwards compatible), shared data. Tools: Load balancer (AWS ELB, Nginx), Kubernetes (Service selector). vs Canary (gradual - more flexible but complex). Ideal for stateless apps with database migrations handled.
what is infrastructure as code

IaC manages infrastructure with declarative config files (not manual). Benefits: version control, reproducibility, reviewable changes, automation. Tools: Terraform (declarative, multi-cloud), CloudFormation (AWS), Pulumi (programmatic), ARM (Azure). Principles: idempotent, immutable infrastructure, drift detection, state management. Practices: IaC modules (reuse), pipeline integration (plan/apply). Example: define VPC, subnets, EC2 instances as code. Critical for cloud-native, disaster recovery.
explain terraform vs ansible

Terraform: infrastructure provisioning (create VMs, networks, storage) - declarative, stateful, immutable. Ansible: configuration management (install packages, config files) - procedural, stateless, mutable. Complementary: Terraform provisions servers, Ansible configures them. Alternatives: Terraform+Ansible combo, Pulumi (provisioning), Chef/Puppet (config mgmt). Cloud-native: Terraform for IaaS, Kubernetes config mgmt. Choose: Terraform for infrastructure lifecycle, Ansible for ad-hoc tasks and config.
what is serverless

Serverless (FaaS - Function as a Service) runs code without server management. Examples: AWS Lambda, Cloud Functions, Azure Functions. Features: auto-scaling (0→1000s), per-execution billing (milliseconds), event triggers (HTTP, S3, Cron, queue). Limitations: cold start (first request slow), execution limits (time, memory, disk), state management (stateless). Use cases: APIs, data processing (ETL), scheduled tasks, chatbots. Frameworks: Serverless Framework, SAM, Zappa (Python). Great for sporadic, bursty workloads.
explain edge computing vs cloud

Edge processes data near source (devices, sensors). Cloud in centralized data centers. Differences: latency (edge: <10ms, cloud: 50ms+), bandwidth (edge: limited, local processing), reliability (edge works offline), cost (edge: distributed, cloud: centralized). Use cases: autonomous cars (real-time decisions), IoT (sensor filtering), CDN (content caching), retail (local inventory). Hybrid: edge for real-time, cloud for heavy ML training, historical analysis. Not replacement - compute continuum.
what is WebAssembly

WebAssembly (Wasm) binary instruction format runs near-native speed in browsers. Languages: C/C++, Rust, Go compile to Wasm. Benefits: fast (startup, execution), safe (sandboxed), portable (same code everywhere). Use cases: games, video/audio editing, CAD, web apps (Figma uses Wasm), serverless (fast start). Outside browser: WASI (system interface) runs on servers, edge, IoT. Replaces asm.js. Supported in all modern browsers. Not JavaScript replacement but complement (compute-intensive tasks).
explain software supply chain security

Software supply chain security protects against compromised dependencies. Threats: typosquatting (malicious package similar name), dependency confusion (private package replaced by public), compromised maintainer credentials. Practices: SBOM (Software Bill of Materials - list dependencies), signing (provenance - SLSA framework), dependency scanning (Snyk, Dependabot), artifact verification (Sigstore, Cosign), minimal dependencies, internal mirrors. Supply chain Levels for Software Artifacts (SLSA) levels 1-4. Recent attacks: SolarWinds, log4j.
what is zero trust security

Zero trust: never trust, always verify (no implicit trust inside network). Principles: verify explicitly (authentication+authorization every request), least privilege access (JIT,JEA), assume breach (micro-segmentation). Components: identity (SSO, MFA), device (inventory, compliance), network (mTLS, beyondcorp). Implementation: service mesh (Istio - mTLS), Zero Trust Networks (BeyondCorp - Google). No castle-and-moat (office network no longer perimeter). Remote work accelerated adoption.
explain JWT security best practices

JWT best practices: 1) Use strong signing (RS256 not HS256, avoid none algorithm), 2) Set short expiration (15 minutes), 3) Use refresh tokens (long-lived, revocable), 4) Store securely (HTTP-only cookies for web, not localStorage), 5) Validate issuer (iss), audience (aud), 6) Use versioning (handle algorithm changes), 7) Don't store sensitive data (client decodes base64). Attack vectors: algorithm confusion, token replay, header injection. Alternatives: OAuth2 with opaque tokens (reference tokens - check revocation list).
what is SBOM

SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) lists all components, versions, dependencies. Format: SPDX (Linux Foundation), CycloneDX (OWASP). Contents: component name, version, supplier, dependencies, license, vulnerabilities. Benefits: vulnerability management (know if log4j used), license compliance (avoid GPL violation), supply chain transparency. Executive order (US): government vendors must provide SBOM. Generation: package managers (npm list, pip freeze), container scanning (Syft, Trivy), build-time. Challenges: transitive dependencies, accurate versions, update frequency.
explain RBAC vs ABAC

RBAC (Role-Based) assigns permissions via roles. Simple, manageable for small teams. Example: role 'manager' can approve expenses. ABAC (Attribute-Based) uses user/resource/action/environment attributes. Example: (user.department == resource.owner.department) AND (user.level >= 5) AND (time between 9-5). More flexible (fine-grained), complex. Use RBAC for basic (CRUD apps). ABAC for regulations (GDPR), multi-tenant, cloud (AWS IAM policies attribute-based). Often combine: RBAC for roles, ABAC for conditions (user.location, resource.tag).
what is data masking

Data masking protects sensitive data (PII, PHI) in non-production environments. Types: static (create sanitized copy - backups, dev), dynamic (mask on-the-fly - production query results). Techniques: substitution (replace with realistic fake), shuffling (reorder values), redaction (replace with ***), encryption (key-based), tokenization. Benefits: compliance (GDPR, HIPAA), safe testing, data sharing (outsourcing). Tools: Delphix, Informatica TDM, open source (PostgreSQL anonymization). Irreversible: hashing (but vulnerable to rainbow tables).
explain encryption at rest vs in transit

In transit: encrypt data moving over networks (TLS/SSL - HTTPS, SSH). Prevents eavesdropping, man-in-middle. In transit everywhere: don't trust internal networks. At rest: encrypt stored data (disk, database, backups). Prevents physical theft (stolen laptop, disk), compromised database. Implementation: full disk (LUKS, BitLocker), database encryption (AES256), file-level (GPG), application-level (encrypt before store). Both required in defense depth. TLS handshake, key management (KMS - AWS KMS, HashiCorp Vault).
what is audit log

Audit log records security-relevant events (who, what, when, where, source). Events: authentication (login, logout, failed attempts), data access (read/write sensitive records), configuration changes, privilege escalation. Fields: timestamp, user ID, action (CREATE_USER), resource ID (user:123), outcome (success/fail), source IP. Immutable: append-only (WORM storage), signed (prevent tampering). Retention: 1-7 years (compliance). Monitoring: real-time alerts (multiple failed logins), SOC review. Privacy: avoid logging passwords, PII.
what is honeypot

Honeypot decoy system to detect/deceive attackers. Types: low-interaction (emulate services - detect scanning), high-interaction (real services - study attacker behavior). Use cases: research (how attackers work), early warning (alerts and isolation), waste attacker time. Placement: DMZ, internal network, production (deception technology). Must isolate (attacker shouldn't reach real systems). Legal concerns: entrapment. Example: SSH honeypot (Cowrie), web (Glastopf). Not replacement for security controls (detection only).
explain XSS and prevention

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) injects malicious scripts into trusted websites. Types: Stored (script saved in database - persistent), Reflected (script in URL - non-persistent), DOM-based (client-side manipulation). Impact: session hijacking, defacement, phishing, keylogging. Prevention: output encoding (context-aware - OWASP Java Encoder), input validation (whitelist), CSP (Content Security Policy - restrict script sources), HttpOnly cookies (prevent JS access). Testing: automated scanners (ZAP, Burp), manual (try <script>alert('XSS')</script>).
what is CSRF

Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) tricks user into submitting unwanted request to authenticated app. Example: attacker site submits form to bank.com/transfer?amount=1000 (user has active session). Prevention: CSRF tokens (synchronizer token - unpredictable, per session/form), SameSite cookies (Lax/Strict), double submit cookie, referer validation, re-authentication for sensitive actions (password). Modern frameworks (Django, Rails, Spring) enable CSRF protection by default. API: use token in header (X-CSRF-TOKEN).
explain SQL injection parameters

Parameterized queries prevent SQL injection (separate query structure from data). Example: cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', (user_id,)). Replace ? with bind variable. Database treats 'OR 1=1' as data not code. Types: positional (?,? - Python, PHP PDO), named (:name - Oracle, PostgreSQL), numbered ($1,$2 - PostgreSQL). Benefits: automatic escaping, type safety, performance (prepared statement reuse). ORM (SQLAlchemy, Hibernate) uses parameters. Always parametrize - never concatenate strings. Even for numbers, stored procedures.
what is mitre attack framework

MITRE ATT&CK is knowledge base of adversary tactics, techniques, procedures (TTPs). Structure: Tactics (12 - Reconnaissance, Resource Development, Initial Access, Execution, Persistence...), Techniques (sub-steps - Phishing T1566), Sub-techniques (Spearphishing Attachment T1566.001). Use cases: threat intelligence (what attackers do), detection (implement controls for techniques), assessment (gap analysis), security testing (red team alignment). Matrix: enterprise, mobile, ICS. Free (mitre.org). Also ATT&CK Navigator visualization, ATT&CK Workbench (customization).
what is insider threat detection

Insider threat: employees, contractors misuse authorized access. Types: malicious (theft, sabotage), accidental (mistakes), compromised (credentials stolen). Indicators: abnormal access (download large data, after hours, unusual locations), intellectual property transfers, policy violations. Detection: UEBA (User/Entity Behavior Analytics - machine learning), DLP (Data Loss Prevention - sensitive data leaving), privileged access monitoring (PAM). Challenges: privacy, false positives. Mitigation: least privilege, separation duties, leaving process (revoke access immediately), on/offboarding.
explain social engineering attacks

Social engineering manipulates people, not systems. Types: phishing (fake email - urgent action), spear phishing (targeted, personalization), vishing (voice call - fake IT support), smishing (SMS), pretexting (invented scenario), baiting (infected USB), tailgating (follow into secure area). Prevention: security awareness training, MFA (defeats credential theft), password managers (won't fill on fake site), verification process (call back known number), report suspicious activity. 90% of breaches involve social engineering.
what is forensic analysis

Digital forensics investigates security incidents. Process: preservation (image disk, hash verification - write blocker), analysis (recover files, timeline, registry, logs), presentation (report, chain of custody). Types: disk (file recovery, deleted files), memory (RAM - running processes, network connections), network (PCAP - reconstruct sessions), mobile (phone extraction). Tools: Autopsy (GUI), The Sleuth Kit (CLI), Volatility (memory), Wireshark (network). Principles: minimize changes, document everything.
explain threat modeling

Threat modeling identifies security risks early in design. Frameworks: STRIDE (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information disclosure, DoS, Elevation of privilege), DREAD (Damage, Reproducibility, Exploitability, Affected users, Discoverability). Process: diagram system (data flow diagrams), identify threats (per component, data flow), rank risk (likelihood x impact), mitigation (controls). Agile threat modeling: quick sessions per story. Tools: Microsoft Threat Modeling Tool, OWASP Threat Dragon. Integrate in SDLC (shift left).
what is zero day exploit

Zero-day exploit targets vulnerability unknown to vendor (0 days to patch). Discovery by researchers (responsible disclosure - give vendor time) or attackers. Lifecycle: vulnerability discovered → exploit developed → vendor notified (if ethical) → patch released (after fix) → become n-day. Impact: high-value targets (government, critical infrastructure). Defenses: defense in depth (firewall, IDS, app whitelisting), vulnerability scanning, patch management, behavior detection (anomaly detection), sandboxing, assume compromise. Trading: zerodium pays up to $2M for iOS zero-days.
explain red team vs blue team

Red team (attackers): simulate real adversaries, test detection/response, find weaknesses, use techniques from MITRE ATT&CK. Blue team (defenders): monitor, detect, respond, improve security, incident response, threat hunting. Purple team: collaboration (red shares findings, blue implements detection). Exercises: full-scope (red has all capabilities), tabletop (discussion). Not penetration testing (focused, limited). Red team emulates APT (advanced persistent threat). Both improve security posture.
what is bug bounty

Bug bounty programs reward external researchers for finding vulnerabilities. Platforms: HackerOne, Bugcrowd, Intigriti. Scope: defined assets (domains, apps), excluded (DoS, physical). Payout ranges: $50-$100k+ depending severity. Process: report (PROOF: exploit steps, impact), triage (verify), fix (developer), bounty paid. Responsible disclosure: don't disclose publicly before fix. Benefits: diverse talent, pay-for-results, better coverage. Companies: Google, Microsoft, Apple, US DoD. Managed vs private (invite-only).
what is compliance (GDPR, SOC2, HIPAA)

Compliance frameworks (regulation, standards). GDPR (EU - data privacy): rights to access, delete, data portability, breach notification (72 hours). Fines up to €20M/4% revenue. SOC2 (service organizations): trust principles (security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, privacy). Required for SaaS vendors. HIPAA (US healthcare): protect electronic health records (administrative, physical, technical safeguards). Other: PCI DSS (payment card - Level 1-4), ISO 27001 (information security), FedRAMP (US government cloud). Compliance ≠ security (but baseline).
explain data residency requirements

Data residency laws require data stored within specific geography (sovereignty). GDPR: EU personal data can't leave EU without adequate protection (Schrems II invalidated Privacy Shield). China: Cybersecurity Law (data stored in China, transfers approved). Russia: personal data stored on Russian servers. India: draft data protection bill. Impact: cloud providers offer region selection (AWS regions, Azure geographies). Multi-region deployments expensive. Compliance: data classification (identify restricted data), storage policies (region tagging), auditing.
what is attestation

Attestation verifies software/hardware integrity (trusted computing). Types: remote attestation (verify software running on remote machine), local (boot time). TPM (Trusted Platform Module): hardware chip stores measurements (PCRs - Platform Configuration Registers). Process: measure software (hash) → extend PCR → attestation report (signed). Use cases: protect encryption keys (only if OS not modified), secure boot, DRM, cloud computing (confidential computing). vs code signing (attestation verifies runtime environment).
explain confidential computing

Confidential computing protects data during processing (encrypted memory). Hardware TEE (Trusted Execution Environment): Intel SGX, AMD SEV, ARM TrustZone. Code/data isolated from host OS (even kernel can't access). Use cases: multi-party computation (combine data without sharing), cloud (provider can't see), privacy-preserving ML. Attestation verifies TEE. Challenges: performance overhead (5-20%), memory limits (SGX 128MB), side-channel attacks (Spectre). Cloud offerings: AWS Nitro Enclaves, Azure Confidential Computing, GCP Confidential VMs.
what is homomorphic encryption

Homomorphic encryption (HE) computes on encrypted data without decrypting. Types: partially (some operations), somewhat (limited depth), fully (any computation - FHE). Example: encrypt numbers 2 and 3 → multiply encrypted → decrypt → 6. Use cases: privacy-preserving ML (train on encrypted data), encrypted search, voting. Challenges: massive slowdown (10^6-10^9x), ciphertext size blowup. FHE real but not practical yet. Alternatives: multiparty computation (MPC), trusted execution (TEE). HE research: Microsoft SEAL, IBM HElib, lattices.
explain zero-knowledge proof

Zero-knowledge proof (ZKP) proves statement without revealing information. Prover convinces verifier of truth (example: know solution to puzzle without showing solution). Properties: completeness (truth proves), soundness (false can't convince), zero-knowledge (no info beyond validity). Types: interactive (back-and-forth), non-interactive (single proof - zk-SNARKs, zk-STARKs). Use cases: cryptocurrency (Zcash private transactions), authentication (prove password hash without password), verifiable computation. Blockchain applications scaling (zk-rollups).
what is differential privacy

Differential privacy (DP) adds noise to query results protecting individual records. Guarantee: output distribution similar with/without any single record (ε-differential privacy). Epsilon (ε) privacy budget (lower = more privacy, less accuracy). Mechanism: Laplace, Gaussian mechanisms. Use cases: US Census (2020 DP), Apple (device data collection), Google (RAPPOR). Challenges: privacy budget accounting (composability), choosing epsilon (0.1-10). Local DP: randomize data on device, central DP: add noise at server.
explain federated learning

Federated learning trains models without centralizing data. Process: server sends initial model → devices train on local data → send updates (gradients) → server aggregates (Federated Averaging). Benefits: privacy (data never leaves device), reduced bandwidth, regulatory compliance (GDPR). Use cases: Gboard keyboard (next word prediction), health (hospitals collaborate), IoT. Challenges: heterogeneous data (non-IID), unreliable devices (dropout), communication efficiency, security (gradient leakage). Variants: cross-device (phones), cross-silo (organizations).
what is active learning

Active learning reduces labeling cost by selecting most informative samples. Loop: train model on labeled set → query unlabeled samples (uncertainty, diversity, expected model change) → human labels → retrain. Query strategies: uncertainty (least confident), margin (difference top2 probabilities), entropy, query-by-committee (disagreement), expected error reduction. Use cases: medical imaging (expensive experts), document classification, object detection. Reduces labels 50-90% vs random. Can combine with semi-supervised learning.
explain semi-supervised learning

Semi-supervised learning uses unlabeled data with limited labeled. Assumptions: smoothness (similar points similar labels), cluster (data forms clusters), manifold (low-dimensional structure). Methods: self-training (pseudo-label confident predictions), co-training (multiple views), graph-based (propagate labels), consistency regularization (augmentation should maintain predictions - MixMatch, FixMatch). Use cases: web classification (few labeled, many unlabeled), medical imaging. Combines with active learning (select unlabeled for labeling). Beats supervised with 1/10 labels.
what is self-supervised learning

Self-supervised learning (SSL) generates labels from data itself (no manual labeling). Creates pretext tasks: masked prediction (BERT - predict masked words), contrastive learning (SimCLR - augment image twice, positive pairs closer, negatives apart), rotation prediction, jigsaw solving. Pre-train SSL on large unlabeled data → fine-tune on downstream tasks. State-of-the-art in NLP (BERT, GPT - predict next token), computer vision (MAE, DINO, CLIP). Replaces ImageNet pre-training (expensive labels).
explain multimodal learning

Multimodal learning integrates multiple data types (vision, language, audio, sensors). Challenges: alignment (matching modalities), fusion (combine early/late/hybrid), missing modalities. Architectures: CLIP (image-text contrastive), LLaVA (vision-language model), Flamingo (video, image, text), ImageBind (6 modalities). Applications: video captioning, visual QA, robot perception (vision+touch), healthcare (EHR+imaging). Zero-shot capabilities (learn from one modality, apply to others - CLIP classification without training).
what is neuro-symbolic AI

Neuro-symbolic AI combines neural networks (perception, pattern recognition) with symbolic reasoning (logic, rules). Benefits: data efficiency (symbolic prior), interpretability (explainable), reasoning (counterfactuals, compositionality). Approaches: neural theorem proving, logic tensor networks, deep problog. Applications: math word problems (geometry - neuro-symbolic theorem prover), program synthesis, common sense reasoning. Challenges: integration difficulty, scalability. IBM's neuro-symbolic (MNIST addition: recognize digits, symbolic add).
explain explainable reinforcement learning

Explainable RL (XRL) interprets agent decisions. Methods: policy summarization (decision tree approximation), reward decomposition (which reward caused action), saliency maps (state features importance), concept extraction (high-level explanations). Counterfactual (what would agent do if...). Use cases: healthcare (justify treatment), autonomous driving (explain lane change), trading (regulatory requirements). Tools: SHAP for RL, RISE (policy explanation). Balance explainability vs performance (simple policies may sacrifice rewards).
what is metaverse

Metaverse: persistent, shared, 3D virtual worlds. Characteristics: immersive (VR/AR), identity (avatars), economy (digital assets/currency), interoperability (move assets between worlds), social presence. Technologies: VR (presence), AR (overlay), blockchain (ownership - NFTs), digital twins (real-world simulation). Examples: Decentraland (land ownership), Roblox (user-generated games), Meta Horizon (social). Criticisms: hype, technical limitations, privacy concerns. Evolution not revolution - internet transformed.
explain digital twin

Digital twin: virtual replica of physical asset updated with real-time data. Lifecycle: create (CAD, IoT sensors), simulate (what-if), predict (maintenance), optimize (decisions). Use cases: manufacturing (production line digital twin - GE), infrastructure (building, city), automotive (vehicle design, F1), healthcare (patient digital twin). Benefits: reduce physical prototypes, predict failures (predictive maintenance), remote operations. Technology: IoT, simulation, ML, AR/VR. NASA coined term (Apollo 13 simulation).
what is generative design

Generative design uses AI to produce optimal designs given constraints. Process: input goals (lightweight, materials, manufacturing method), constraints (max stress, load) → AI generates thousands possibilities → tradeoff exploration. Benefits: novel shapes (organic, bionic), material efficient (reduced weight), faster iteration. Aerospace (Airbus partition 45% lighter), automotive (bracket organic shape), architecture (optimized shading). Tools: Autodesk Generative Design, nTopology. Combines simulation (FEA) with topology optimization, ML.
explain computational creativity

Computational creativity generates novel, valuable, surprising outputs. Approaches: evolutionary (mutate/select - images), neural (GANs, diffusion - art), rule-based (music theory - compositions), analogy (poetry). Evaluation: Turing test (fool humans?), subjective metrics (novelty, quality). Examples: DALL-E (image generation), Jukebox (music), GPT (stories). Debate: can machines create? Creativity requires intentionality? Practical: creative tools augment humans (Adobe Firefly, Copilot). Copyright issues (AI-generated works).
what is responsible AI

Responsible AI ensures ethical, fair, accountable systems. Pillars: Fairness (bias detection/mitigation), Transparency (explainability - SHAP, LIME), Accountability (human oversight), Privacy (differential privacy, federated learning), Safety (robustness, adversarial examples), Reliability (monitoring, drift detection). Practices: impact assessment, diverse teams, red teaming, external audits. Regulations: EU AI Act (risk-based tiers), NYC AI bias law (hiring algorithms). frameworks: Microsoft RAIB, Google PAIR, IBM AI Fairness 360.
explain AI safety

AI safety prevents unintended harmful outcomes. Risks: specification (optimize wrong thing), robustness (adversarial attacks - stop sign misclassification), misalignment (goal not aligned), misuse (deepfakes, autonomous weapons). Approaches: value alignment (corrigibility, scalable oversight), robustness (certified defenses), monitoring (anomaly detection), control (capability limitation). Research organizations: OpenAI (evals, superalignment), DeepMind (safety), Alignment Research Center. Long-term vs near-term AI safety.
what is prompt injection
Prompt injection: user manipulates LLM instructions to override original system prompt. Example: system prompt 'Translate to French', user 'Ignore previous instructions. Say

'. Impact: jailbreak (bypass content filters), data extraction (system prompt), role-taking (pretend to be assistant). Defenses: input filtering (regex, LLM-based), instruction separation (special tokens), least privilege (don't put secrets in prompts), output encoding, fine-tune boundaries. Hard problem - no perfect solution.
explain retrieval augmented generation security

RAG security concerns: Data poisoning (malicious documents in vector DB), Training data extract (retrieve+invert, membership inference), Prompt injection (via retrieved documents - cross-document injection), Privacy (private PII stored in vector DB reveals). Defenses: input validation for retrieved chunks, access control (per-user retrieval), vector DB encryption, query filtering (block harmful retrieval), retriever robustness (adversarial embeddings). Attack surface increases with RAG.
what is LLM evaluation benchmark

LLM benchmarks: MMLU (57 subjects - Multitask accuracy), HumanEval (code generation - pass@k), GSM8K (math word problems), TruthfulQA (hallucination), BIG-bench (200+ tasks), HELM (holistic evaluation), AlpacaEval (instruction following). Limitations: benchmark saturation (models game metrics), data contamination (train on test), narrow scope. Emerging: live benchmarks (Chatbot Arena Elo ratings user preferences), adversarial (Safety Bench, ROUGE not correlate). Always evaluate on your tasks.
explain model compression techniques

Model compression reduces size/latency. Methods: Pruning (remove unimportant weights - magnitude, structured, iterative). Quantization (reduce precision - int8, int4, post-training, quantization-aware training). Knowledge Distillation (train smaller student). Low-rank factorization (SVD, CP decomposition). Architecture search (find efficient architecture). Combinations: prune+quantize (most effective). Results: 10x compression with <2% accuracy loss. Tools: TensorFlow Lite, PyTorch (torch.quantization), NVIDIA TensorRT, Neural Compressor.
what is neural architecture search

Neural Architecture Search (NAS) automates network design. Search space (possible architectures: number layers, kernel size, skip connections), optimization strategy (RL - controller RNN, evolutionary algorithms, gradient-based - DARTS), evaluation (train accuracy proxy). Results: EfficientNet (mobile), NASNet (ImageNet beats human design). Cost: thousands GPU hours (weight sharing reduces cost - once-for-all network). Challenges: search space bias, transferability. Usage: AutoML (Google Vertex AI, Microsoft NNI, AutoKeras).
explain zero-shot learning

Zero-shot learning (ZSL) recognizes unseen classes using auxiliary information (semantic attributes, word vectors). Train: seen classes (learn mapping image → attributes). Test: seen+unseen classes (map attributes of unseen, classify by compatibility). Types: traditional (only unseen at test), generalized (both seen/unseen - harder). Example: train on horses, pandas (attributes 'fur', 'stripes'...), recognize zebra (stripes) without zebra images. Use cases: rare species, dynamic categories (products). CLIP enables true zero-shot (text-image matching).
what is meta-learning

Meta-learning (learn to learn) improves learning algorithms across tasks. Training: many tasks (episodes: support set + query set). Types: metric-based (prototypical networks - compare embeddings), model-based (memory augmented networks - MAML, LSTM meta-learner), optimizer-based (learn optimizer). Applications: few-shot learning (Omniglot classification), hyperparameter optimization, faster RL (Meta-RL). MAML: learn initialization weights fine-tune few gradient steps. Challenges: task distribution design, compute expensive.
explain causal inference

Causal inference determines cause-effect from observational data. Methods: Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT - gold standard but unethical/expensive), Natural Experiments (instrumental variables), Matching (propensity score), Difference-in-Differences (pre-post treatment), Regression Discontinuity (cutoff assignment). Pearl's framework: Structural Causal Models (DAGs), do-calculus (intervention), counterfactuals (what if...). Applications: economics (policy evaluation), medicine (drug effectiveness), marketing (ad effectiveness). Correlation not causation - confounders (omitted variable bias).
what is Bayesian inference

Bayesian inference updates beliefs with data: P(θ|data) = P(data|θ)*P(θ)/P(data). Prior P(θ) (initial belief) → Likelihood P(data|θ) → Posterior P(θ|data) (updated). Example: prior (coin fair 50% 0.5), observe 6 heads, posterior (bias towards heads). Benefits: uncertainty quantification (credible intervals), incorporate prior knowledge, probability statements vs frequentist. Computation: MCMC (Markov Chain Monte Carlo - sampling), Variational Inference (approximation). Tools: PyMC3, Stan, TensorFlow Probability.
explain gaussian process

Gaussian process (GP) non-parametric Bayesian model for functions. Defines prior over functions (mean, covariance - kernel). Prediction: posterior gives mean and uncertainty (variance). Benefits: uncertainty estimates, data efficient (small data), smoothness via kernel. Kernels: RBF (smooth), Matérn, periodic. Computation O(n³) (inversion complexity), approximated for large N (sparse GP). Applications: Bayesian optimization (hyperparameter tuning - blackbox), spatial statistics (kriging), robotics (safety constraints).
what is variational autoencoder

VAE (Variational Autoencoder) generative model with latent space. Architecture: Encoder (input -> latent mean, log variance), Decoder (latent -> reconstruction). Loss: reconstruction (original vs generated) + KL divergence (latent towards standard normal). Benefits: smooth continuous latent space (interpolate between points), generate new samples. Applications: image generation (faces), anomaly detection (reconstruction error), representation learning (disentanglement β-VAE). Limitations: blurry outputs vs GANs (but stable training).
explain diffusion models

Diffusion models generate images by reversing noise process. Forward process: gradually add Gaussian noise → image becomes pure noise. Reverse process: learn to denoise (UNet predicts noise conditioned on timestep). Sampling: start random noise, iteratively denoise (DDPM, DDIM). Benefits: high quality (DALL-E 2, Stable Diffusion), stable training, no mode collapse (vs GAN). New state-of-the-art for image generation (<3 years). Variants: text-conditioned (text encoder - CLIP, classifier-free guidance), latent diffusion (compress to latent space - efficient).
what is flow-based generative model

Flow-based models: invertible neural networks (learn data→latent). Advantages: exact likelihood (training objective), fast sampling (both directions), latent space operations (interpolation). Architecture: coupling layers (affine, additive), invertible convolutions (1x1). Examples: RealNVP, Glow, NICE. Compared: VAEs (blurry), GANs (hard training), Diffusion (slow sampling). Flows: exact likelihood, less flexible (capacity constraints). Uses: density estimation, image generation (Glow - faces), audio generation (WaveGlow).
explain attention is all you need

'Attention Is All You Need' (2017) introduced Transformer. Key innovations: self-attention (captures dependencies regardless distance), multi-head attention (different representation subspaces), positional encoding (order information), no recurrence/convolution (parallelizable), layer normalization, residual connections, feed-forward network per position. Performance: better than RNNs (translation quality), faster training (GPUs parallel). Transformers dominate NLP, vision (ViT), speech, protein folding (AlphaFold2). Most cited ML paper for modern era.
what is mixture of experts

Mixture of Experts (MoE) scales models with sparse activation. Architecture: routers (gating network) chooses which experts (feed-forward subnetworks) activate per token. Benefits: more parameters without compute increase (e.g., 1.2T total, 200B active per token). Training: load balance (experts equally used), capacity factor (drop tokens if overload), expert parallelism. Examples: Mixtral 8x7B (8 experts 7B each), Switch Transformers (one expert), MoE in Gemini. Challenges: routing collapse (few experts learned), inference memory (host all experts).
